questions.question: What is the requirement for an object to remain at a constant temperature in a room?
options: It must transfer energy away at the same rate that it receives energy.
It must stop emitting radiation entirely.
It must be a perfect reflector of all incident radiation.
It must be at the same density as the surrounding air.
questions.explanation:
Thermal equilibrium (constant temperature) is achieved when the rate of energy absorption equals the rate of energy emission. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking constant temperature means no energy is being exchanged.

questions.question: If the Earth receives energy from the Sun at a higher rate than it radiates energy back into space, what is the consequence?
options: The temperature of the Earth will increase.
The temperature of the Earth will decrease.
The Earth will move further away from the Sun.
The rate of nuclear fusion in the Sun will decrease.
questions.explanation:
A net gain in energy (input > output) leads to an increase in the internal energy and thus a rise in temperature. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the Earth would naturally 'vent' the extra energy without a temperature change.

questions.question: A vacuum flask has silvered internal surfaces. What is the specific purpose of this design feature?
options: To reduce thermal energy transfer by radiation.
To reduce thermal energy transfer by conduction.
To prevent the formation of convection currents.
To increase the structural strength of the glass walls.
questions.explanation:
The silvered surfaces reflect infrared radiation back towards the source, minimizing energy loss or gain via radiation. 

**Common mistake:** Believing the silvering blocks conduction; that is the job of the vacuum.

questions.question: Space blankets are thin, shiny sheets used to keep people warm. Why are they effective even though they are very thin?
options: They reflect the person's own body radiation back towards them.
They are made of a material with extremely high specific heat capacity.
They trap a thick layer of air to stop conduction.
They generate thermal energy through a chemical reaction with skin.
questions.explanation:
The shiny material acts as a reflector, sending emitted body heat back to the person instead of letting it escape to the surroundings. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the blanket 'warms' the person by creating heat.

questions.question: Which row in the table correctly identifies factors affecting the rate of thermal energy transfer by radiation?
options: Surface Temperature: Yes; Surface Area: Yes; Surface Colour: Yes
Surface Temperature: Yes; Surface Area: No; Surface Colour: Yes
Surface Temperature: No; Surface Area: Yes; Surface Colour: Yes
Surface Temperature: Yes; Surface Area: Yes; Surface Colour: No
questions.explanation:
The source material identifies color, texture, surface area, and surface temperature as the primary factors. 

**Common mistake:** Forgetting that surface area plays a role in how much radiation can be emitted per second.
